result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a sentence finds its shape. Not the blank page, but the instant when the page is still empty yet already humming, the way a valley hums when the wind is deciding which canyon to rush through. There is a quality of almost—that keening, half-lit place where language has not yet agreed to be language, where the words are still wild electrons orbiting a nucleus of impulse. I try to cup that moment in my hands the way a child traps a firefly: careful, trembling, sure that the light will escape if I press too hard. Most nights I fail. The sentence streaks off into the dark, and I’m left blinking at after-trails. But sometimes, very late, when the house is breathing in its sleep and even the refrigerator has stopped its small mechanical hymn, the glow stays put. I can screw the lid on the jar, poke holes in the metal so the thing can breathe, and pretend I’ve domesticated mystery.

I wonder if that’s what we’re all doing—building leaky jars for the sake of saying we caught something. We marry, label ourselves with job titles, file taxes, buy couch insurance, as though any of those gestures will hold the chaos at arm’s length. Meanwhile the chaos curls up at the foot of the bed like a golden retriever that has no plans to leave. It licks our feet while we dream of sheep counters who are also tax attorneys. In the morning we drink coffee that tastes faintly of obedience and pretend not to notice the dog hair on our socks.

When I was eight I believed the moon followed me personally. Not the generic version that followed everyone, but a private moon, stitched to my shadow by an invisible red thread. I tested it by running downhill, arms helicoptering, convinced that if I reached traffic-light speed the thread would snap and the moon would collide with the Methodist church steeple. The steeple would crumple like a soda can, and the moon would roll into the duck pond, a vast silver bowling ball with my name engraved on it. The pastor would emerge in his pajamas, rubbing the grain of sleep from his eyes, and demand to know which parishioner had lost their planetary body. I rehearsed an apology: “Sorry, sir, it got away from me.” I pictured him sighing, then borrowing the janitor’s push-broom to coax the moon back into the sky, same way my mother coaxed the cat off the picnic table with a broom that smelled of forsythia and unspoken curse words.

The moon never did crash. The thread held. Instead, I crashed—into adolescence, into acne like a field of miniature volcanoes, into the realization that my parents’ voices could crack the way ice cracks before the pond gives way. One February night I stood at the window while my father explained to my mother that the factory was “downsizing,” a word that sounded to me like a villain in a space opera. He held the word the way you hold a dead bird you found on the porch: softly, worried that rigor mortis might be contagious. My mother stared at the kitchen table as if reading future bills in the oak grain. Outside, my moon kept vigil, disinterested, rotating slowly like a lamp in a shop window nobody can afford to enter.

Years later, in a city that smelled of wet pavement and cilantro, I dated a woman who collected broken umbrellas. She hung them from her ceiling like upside-down bats with aluminum ribs. On rainy evenings we lay beneath them listening to the ghost taps of droplets hitting fabric long gone. She told me rain was just the sky apologizing for centuries of behavior it couldn’t amend. I told her that must be why puddles never lasted—they were acceptance letters no one believed. We made love while the surviving umbrellas creaked above us, a nautical chorus of stranded masts. After she left—for a job in Singapore, for someone who understood cryptocurrency—I kept one umbrella, turquoise with a tear shaped like the state of Ohio. I never opened it, but sometimes I carried it under my arm the way poets carry unread poems to parties, hoping someone would ask why. No one ever did. Umbrellas, like broken hearts, are expected to stay shut in public.

There is a small bakery near the train tracks that sells bread so hot it sings. I mean literally: the crust crackles in frequencies that translate, if you listen with your mouth open, into a lullaby in a language on the verge of extinction. The baker, whose name might be Magda or might be Magdalena depending on how much sleep she’s had, claims the recipe arrived in a dream carried by a stork with a missing toe. She woke up with the taste of ash and honey on her tongue, and by sunset the first loaf had emerged from the oven weeping steam like a repentant dragon. I buy a loaf every Friday, break it on the sidewalk so the pigeons can partake of ancestral sorrow and ancestral sweetness. They coo in minor keys. Their eyes reflect departing boxcars tagged with graffiti that reads EITHER/OR in neon pink. I pretend it’s a message from the universe, but it’s probably just someone’s philosophy major cousin killing a summer.

What I’m circling is the idea that everything leaks. Memory, roofs, the supposedly airtight container of the self. We patch with stories, with vinyl caulk, with denial, yet some phantom draft still slips through, carrying the scent of a classroom from 1993—chalk dust, bologna sandwiches, the teacher’s lilac deodorant failing to mask the sadness of unmarried aunts. You can be riding a subway at rush hour, wedged between a man eating hard-boiled eggs and a woman whose earbuds emit tinny sitar, when suddenly the third-grade cloakroom materializes. You feel the plastic hook beneath your fingers, the one that held your navy-blue coat with the torn pocket. You smell wet wool. You hear the bully named Travis snickering because you wrote “I love my mom” on your sneakers. The train brakes. Fluorescent lights flicker. You are eight and thirty-eight simultaneously, a double exposure, a loaf of bread singing about its own consumption.

Maybe that’s why I keep writing: not to capture the firefly but to watch its escape routes. Each sentence constructs a door; each period installs a peephole. Look through and you might catch the moon tugging at its thread, the umbrellas practicing flight, the pigeons rehearsing a requiem in a minor key only loaves understand. The draft under the door is the real protagonist. It carries weather from other lives, it smells of crayons and Singapore rain and my father’s factory badge. I could try to seal it with rolled-up towels, but then how would the night slip in to remind me it’s unfinished? How would the next sentence arrive, barefoot, panting, smelling of wet cement and forgiveness?

So I leave the gap. I sit beside it with a notebook open to the undefined hour. Somewhere a stork lands on one foot, confused by the scent of hot bread. Somewhere a woman in an office tower looks out at the equatorial storm and thinks of umbrellas she never opened. Somewhere my eight-year-old self keeps running downhill, arms out, thread glowing like a fiber-optic promise. The moon winks, complicit. The draft combs my hair with cool fingers. I write: In the beginning was the leak, and the leak was with us, and we were the leak. The page dries. The firefly blinks twice, then proceeds to rewrite itself into a constellation shaped like an apology I will spend the rest of my life learning to pronounce.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
